metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `validate_packaged_routing()` is updated to accept `exec:DET` routing entries (e.g. by checking `entry.get('exec','').upper() == 'DET'` as a valid non-LLM entry exempt from the CANONICAL_LLM membership check), so adding DET project criteria to `criteria_routing.json` doesn't break the CI parity gate.
findings.finding: `validate_packaged_routing()` is a CI gate that flags any key in `criteria_routing.json` (packaged) not in `CANONICAL_LLM` as an orphan. The plan proposes adding `detector` + `fail_mode` fields to DET routing entries in `criteria_routing.json` and moving the security-class rows there data-drivenly. The existing security-class entries (`secret-detection`, `high-critical-security`) already carry `exec: DET` in the code-review routing — but in plan-review's `criteria_routing.json` there are NO DET entries today, and `validate_packaged_routing` would reject any added project DET entry as an orphan (it is not in `CANONICAL_LLM`). The plan does not specify how the CI parity gate is updated to accept `exec:DET` project criteria entries.
findings.suggested_fix: In `validate_packaged_routing`, before the orphan check, skip (or separately validate) entries whose `exec` is `'DET'` — they are legitimate non-LLM entries that will never be in `CANONICAL_LLM`. Alternatively, add a `CANONICAL_DET_ROUTING` set (analogous to `CANONICAL_DET`) for the built-in DET routing entries and extend the orphan check to accept members of either set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `attest.registry_version()` needs to incorporate the overlay content-signature (or `effective_routing()` hash) so that a change to a project DET criterion or its `fail_mode` invalidates existing attestations.
findings.finding: `attest.registry_version()` stamps `CANONICAL_DET` + `CANONICAL_LLM` + `_routing_index()` (the PACKAGED routing, not `effective_routing()`). After this story, activated project DET criteria live in the overlay (`effective_routing()`) but are invisible to `registry_version()`. A project that adds a new DET invariant, or changes a detector rule, will not invalidate existing attestations — the drift-refresh tripwire silently passes stale attestations as if the project's DET criteria never changed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the exec:DET exclusion to `production_batch_runner._project_criteria` (line 199 area): skip any descriptor where `str(desc.get('exec','')).upper() == 'DET'` before tier-splitting, consistent with the plan's direct-string-check instruction; also specify whether `exec_tier()` itself should be updated to return a distinct 'DET' tier to prevent future regressions.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the exec:DET LLM-batch exclusion at two sites (orchestrator.route_criteria and workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria) but misses a third live exclusion site: production_batch_runner.py:199 calls `registry.exec_tier(desc) == 'AGENT'` to tier-split criteria into LLM batch lists. The live exec_tier() (registry.py:519-525) has NO DET arm and silently remaps exec:DET → '1-TURN', so a DET criterion passing through _project_criteria() is admitted to the single-turn LLM batch. The plan's instruction to filter via direct string check (not exec_tier()) is not applied to this path.
findings.location: 'exec:DET criteria never enter the LLM batch' section (remediation, run 4); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/production_batch_runner.py:199

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the choice to have `run_det_floor` call `registry.effective_criteria()` internally rather than accepting extra checks as a parameter from the caller (e.g. `run_det_floor(ctx, project_checks=())`) — or adopt the parameter approach to preserve the DET floor's current isolation from the LLM-tier registry.
findings.finding: `det_floor.py` currently has zero imports from `plan_review/registry.py` — the DET floor is architecturally isolated from the LLM-tier registry by design (it imports only stdlib, dataclasses, logging, and the grounding oracle lazily per-check). The plan's second phase adds a runtime `det_floor.run_det_floor` → `registry.effective_criteria()` call, creating a novel cross-module dependency. No justification is given for this coupling vs the simpler alternative of having `run_det_floor(ctx, extra_checks=())` accept the caller-discovered checks as a parameter, keeping the floor isolated and the discovery logic in the orchestrator/workflow layer where the registry is already imported.
findings.location: ## Static P1–P9 + dynamic project-check phase (remediation, run 2)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the signature to `run_det_floor(ctx: PlanContext, project_checks: Sequence[Callable[[PlanContext], DetResult]] = ()) -> list[DetResult]` and move the `registry.effective_criteria()` discovery + scan-consumer construction into `workflow_ops` (where the registry is already imported), passing the built checks in. The floor stays isolated; the orchestration layer owns discovery.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit AC checklist items covering: (a) exec:DET exclusion in both orchestrator.route_criteria and workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria; (b) the filter uses direct dict check not exec_tier(); (c) detector selector shape {id?, id_prefix?} validated in _validate_routing_entry; (d) packaged security rows carry explicit fail_mode:"closed"; (e) run_security_detectors alias still returns same result as run_detectors; and (f) all four named tests exist and pass.
findings.finding: The 'DET-tier isolation, thin-slice sequencing & schema details' section (remediation run 4) describes multiple concrete deliverables — specifically (a) the exec:DET exclusion filter in orchestrator.route_criteria AND workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria, (b) the rationale for reading exec=='DET' directly from the dict vs via exec_tier(), (c) the detector selector shape {id?, id_prefix?}, (d) the fail_mode default-safe guarantee (explicit 'closed' in packaged routing), (e) the run_security_detectors→run_detectors rename+alias, and (f) four named tests (fail_mode branching, three-way scoping, alias equivalence, DET-not-in-LLM-batch) — yet none of these has a covering acceptance criterion. The run 4 section has zero AC checklist items of its own.
findings.location: ## DET-tier isolation, thin-slice sequencing & schema details (remediation, run 4)
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to ## Acceptance Criteria (remediation additions, run 2) or add a new ## Acceptance Criteria (run 4) block:
- [ ] `orchestrator.route_criteria` and `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria` both filter out `exec == "DET"` descriptors; the filter reads the descriptor dict directly (not via `exec_tier()`); a test asserts an activated DET criterion appears in DET-floor results and is absent from the LLM `include_*` / route_criteria set.
- [ ] `_validate_routing_entry` accepts and validates `detector: {id?, id_prefix?}`; the packaged security routing rows carry explicit `fail_mode: "closed"`.
- [ ] `run_security_detectors` alias returns the same result as `run_detectors`; a test asserts this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `check_registry_coverage` and `validate_packaged_routing` are updated (or explicitly scoped) to handle `exec:DET` entries correctly — either by skipping the `get_prompt` call for DET ids, or by documenting them as an expected category that the completeness guard treats as 'covered by the DET phase, not the prompt library'.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'expand-contract (prompt-backed callers unaffected)' for the `_descriptor_from_prompt` DET branch, but `check_registry_coverage` (registry.py:501-516) unconditionally calls `_descriptor_from_prompt` for every id returned by `effective_criteria()`. When a DET project criterion is activated, it enters `effective_criteria()` and `check_registry_coverage` will call `_descriptor_from_prompt` for it — without the DET branch, this raises (no prompt file exists) and the criterion is reported as 'missing'. The completeness guard is a prompt-backed caller that IS affected.
findings.location: ## What — DET fallback descriptor shape; AC line 'DET criteria build a descriptor without a prompt'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the exact accessor for the detector rule message in the DET descriptor branch — confirm it is `det.rule.get('message', det.id)` (with a fallback), not a `.message` attribute, since `Detector` has no such property; also confirm the SARIF-backend sentinel has a usable message field.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `scenario=detector rule message` for the DET fallback descriptor, implying `Detector.message` is an accessible attribute — but the `Detector` dataclass (grounding/detectors/registry.py lines 88-152) has no `.message` property. The human-readable rule message lives at `det.rule.get('message')` (inside the raw rule dict), not as a first-class `Detector` attribute. An implementer following the plan literally will get an AttributeError.
findings.location: ## What — DET fallback descriptor shape; 'Schema + migration details' section (remediation, run 4)
findings.suggested_fix: In the DET _descriptor_from_prompt branch, use `det.rule.get('message') or routing.get('name') or cid` as the `scenario` value; document that SARIF-backend detectors (e.g. the gitleaks sentinel) may lack a rule-level message and need the envelope or routing name as fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the descriptor field name conflict: canonicalize on `scenario` (consistent with the live schema at registry.py:353 and the consumer at passes.py:174) and remove the contradictory 'rubric' reference in thin-slice step 2.
findings.finding: The DET descriptor field for the detector rule message is named 'scenario' in the 'What' section ('scenario=detector rule message') but 'rubric' in thin-slice sequencing step 2 ('rubric = detector rule message'). The live descriptor schema uses 'scenario' (registry.py:353). An implementer reading both sections gets contradictory field names for the same key.
findings.location: 'What' section (DET fallback descriptor shape) vs 'Thin-slice sequencing + coherence' section step 2 (remediation, run 4)
findings.suggested_fix: In the thin-slice sequencing step 2 description, replace 'rubric = detector rule message' with 'scenario = detector rule message (the existing descriptor field name)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact accessor for the detector rule message in the DET descriptor branch: `det.rule.get('message', det.id)` (or equivalent), since `Detector` has no `.message` attribute.
findings.finding: The plan says the DET descriptor sets 'scenario=detector rule message', but the live Detector dataclass (detectors/registry.py) has NO .message property — message is stored at det.rule.get('message'). The plan never specifies the property access path, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.location: 'What' section — DET fallback descriptor shape; 'Schema + migration details' section step 2
findings.suggested_fix: In the descriptor shape spec, write: 'scenario = det.rule.get("message") or det.id  # message lives in the rule dict, not as a Detector property'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider formally splitting into at least two child tickets (e.g., child-1: data-driven routing + fail_mode in code_review/detectors.py; child-2: DET descriptor branch + run_det_floor phase + LLM-batch exclusion + plan-review integration + ADR) so each child is independently mergeable and verifiable.
findings.finding: The ticket exceeds the epic/parent threshold on multiple axes simultaneously (>3 files, ≥3 layers, new interface, >6 acceptance criteria across 8+ checkbox items) yet has no child tickets — the internal sequencing helps but does not substitute for formally decomposable, independently-closable units.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all remediation runs combined) — 8+ checkbox ACs; Scope section listing 4+ files across 3+ layers
findings.suggested_fix: Child-1 (MVP): criteria_routing.json schema + `run_detectors` rename + security class preserved fail-closed — proven by existing test_code_review_ws5.py. Child-2: descriptor branch + run_det_floor dynamic phase + DET-tier isolation + plan-review scoping + ADR. Each child closes independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Centralize the `exec:DET` exclusion into a single shared predicate (e.g., `is_llm_routable(descriptor) -> bool` in a shared module) imported by all routing sites, so future sites cannot accidentally omit the filter.
findings.finding: The `exec:DET` LLM-batch exclusion filter is specified at two distinct routing sites (`orchestrator.route_criteria` and `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria`) as independent inline checks, rather than via a shared predicate (e.g., `is_llm_routable(descriptor)`). If a future routing site omits the filter, DET descriptors silently enter the LLM batch and are handed to `pass1_chunk` with no prompt — a silent failure mode the ADR alone cannot prevent.
findings.location: DET-tier isolation section — 'exec:DET criteria never enter the LLM batch'; AC 'orchestrator.route_criteria skips … workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria's effective vocabulary excludes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `def is_llm_routable(descriptor: dict) -> bool: return str(descriptor.get('exec', '')).upper() != 'DET'` to a shared location (e.g., `rebar/llm/routing_utils.py`) and have both `orchestrator.route_criteria` and `workflow_ops.plan_review_assemble_criteria` call it; the ADR references this function as the enforcement point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism that marks a DET routing entry (or detector) as 'diff-inherent / N/A at plan time' — e.g. add a `plan_scoping: diff_only` boolean field to the `criteria_routing.json` DET entry schema (validated in `_validate_routing_entry`) so the plan-review consumer can branch deterministically and the AC test can set it to true/false in fixtures.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a three-way plan-time DET scoping (file_impact-scoped / advisory / N/A-fail-open) and requires a test for the 'diff-needed → N/A' path, but never specifies a mechanism by which the consumer identifies a criterion as 'diff-inherent'. No routing-entry field, detector property, naming convention, or auto-detection rule is described, so an implementer must invent the identification mechanism — a real design choice left open.
findings.location: Plan-review consumer integration (remediation, run 1) — 'N/A at plan time (fail-open)' bullet; Schema + migration details (remediation, run 4) — 'diff-needed→N/A' test assertion
findings.suggested_fix: Add `plan_scoping: {enum: ["file_impact", "advisory", "diff_only"], default: "file_impact"}` to the DET routing-entry schema (documented in run-4 schema details and the ADR). The plan-review consumer reads this field: `file_impact` → scope to declared files; `advisory` → always advisory; `diff_only` → skip=N/A (abstain). `_validate_routing_entry` rejects unknown values. This makes the three-way scoping test fully deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test asserting `_validate_routing_entry` raises/rejects on an invalid `fail_mode` value and on a `detector` selector missing both `id` and `id_prefix`.
findings.finding: The plan calls out `_validate_routing_entry` gains `fail_mode ∈ {open, closed}` validation and a `detector` selector shape (`id` vs `id_prefix`), but names no test for invalid/malformed routing entries (e.g., unrecognized fail_mode value, detector selector with both fields absent) — a new validation code path with no specified failure-mode test.
findings.location: Schema + migration details section; `_validate_routing_entry` mentions; AC: 'fail_mode ∈ {open, closed} validation'
findings.suggested_fix: In the test module for routing schema validation, add a parametrized test: `@pytest.mark.parametrize('entry,exc', [({'fail_mode': 'strict', 'detector': {'id': 'x'}}, ValidationError), ({'fail_mode': 'open', 'detector': {}}, ValidationError)])` and assert `_validate_routing_entry(entry)` raises.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete `detector` selector schema: specify that exactly one of `id` or `id_prefix` must be present for `exec: DET` entries, that `_validate_routing_entry` rejects a DET entry missing both, and that having both is also an error.
findings.finding: The `detector` selector sub-object introduced on DET routing entries ('detector: {id?: str, id_prefix?: str}') has an underspecified schema: whether both keys are optional simultaneously (what happens when neither is present), whether the object itself is required for exec:DET entries, and how the consumer resolves when both id and id_prefix are set are all undefined. The _validate_routing_entry extension is promised but its logic is not specified.
findings.location: 'Schema + migration details' section (remediation, run 4) — 'detector selector shape'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit existing documentation, docstrings, and inline comments for references to `run_security_detectors` and update them to reference `run_detectors` (noting the alias for backward compat), or confirm no such references exist outside the implementation file itself.
findings.finding: The plan renames run_security_detectors to run_detectors (keeping a deprecated alias) but does not check for or address stale references to the old name in existing documentation, comments, or READMEs. The ADR deliverable covers new concepts but is silent on whether existing docs reference run_security_detectors by name.
findings.location: Schema + migration details (clears T8/T4/E1/E5 advisories) — rename subsection

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one end-to-end validation mechanism — e.g., a dogfooding AC that activates a sample `.rebar/detectors/` rule against a fixture repo and asserts it surfaces as a finding in a simulated plan-review or code-review run — so the 'project team can use custom invariants' value is concretely verifiable.
findings.finding: No concrete end-to-end validation mechanism (dogfooding scenario, before/after workflow comparison, or operational signal) is present. All ACs are unit-test assertions over internal system state; no AC demonstrates a real project team actually activating a project DET invariant and observing it block/pass in a live gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A fixture repo with one activated project DET criterion (a banned-import rule) produces a blocking DetResult in both plan-review (file_impact-scoped) and code-review (diff-scoped) when the target file is present; a repo with no project DET criteria is byte-identical to today.' This exercises the full consumer path from .rebar/detectors/ rule → gate result, not just internal routing logic.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
